summary: DeVault examines the controversy around 'real name' policies in free software projects like Linux. He acknowledges the legitimate legal rationale behind such policies—establishing copyright provenance and accountability through mechanisms like the DCO—while detailing the harm they cause to marginalized groups, harassment victims, and transgender contributors. He proposes a middle path: using the DCO for provenance while letting contributors participate under whatever identity they feel most comfortable with, and supporting name changes in historical records.
key_points: Real name policies have a legitimate legal basis: establishing copyright provenance and ensuring contributors accept liability for the code they submit
These policies disproportionately exclude marginalized demographics, harassment victims, and transgender people who face deadnaming
The framing as purely discriminatory misses that maintainers face real legal liability when accepting anonymous contributions
A workable compromise is using the DCO while allowing contributors to participate under their preferred identity rather than requiring legal names
When a contributor's identity changes, projects should make every effort to update contemporary, future, and historical records
money_quotes: A maintainer whose 'real name' policy is challenged on this basis would also be reasonable in saying 'I feel for you, but I cannot agree to accept legal liability over the provenance of this code, nor can I communicate that risk to end-users who acquire code under a license that may or may not be valid as such'.

The profile of the contributor who is comfortable using their 'real name' is likely to fall more narrowly into over-represented demographics in our community; enforcing a real-name policy will ostracize some people.
Those who feel marginalized by 'real name' policies often skew young, and many treat matters such as copyright and licensing with disdain.
Addressing a trans person by their old name, or 'deadname', is highly uncomfortable. Doing so deliberately, as a matter of policy or otherwise, is a form of discrimination.
The truth is, managing the names of participants is more challenging than anyone would like.

I encourage people to utilize an identity they use beyond the project's walls, to foster a social environment and a connection to the broader community, to establish accountability, and to ensure that participants are reachable for further discussion on their work.
key_insight: Real name policies have legitimate legal motivations but cause real harm to marginalized contributors; the solution is to establish provenance through the DCO while letting people participate under their preferred identity.
